sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Tyler, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing.
He describes a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is painful and requires him to wash it down with liquids.
He has never fully choked but has thought about potentially choking.
The difficulty swallowing is more pronounced with solids, particularly larger or harder foods.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He has been taking metformin 500mg twice daily for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
Review of systems: The patient denies any coughing, vomiting blood, or blood in his stools.
He has not experienced any weight loss, chest pain, or shortness of breath.
He has some epigastric tenderness but no masses or significant swelling.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice daily for diabetes
Norvasc (dose not specified) for high blood pressure
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/50

Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine with no swelling, thyroid feels normal, no masses or lymph nodes in the neck, normal lung exam, normal heart exam, normal back exam, normal extremity exam, and normal neurological exam.
The throat exam shows no masses, swelling, or redness.
The epigastric tenderness is noted.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has difficulty swallowing due to a possible narrowing in the throat, which may be caused by a stricture or narrowing of the esophagus.
Problem list: Dysphagia; Stricture of the esophagus; Diabetes mellitus; Hypertension; Reflux disease
Plan:
I prescribed prilosec 20mg once daily for reflux disease.
I recommended that the patient chew their foods frequently, especially hard or large foods, to avoid getting them stuck.
I also recommended that the patient call the office if they experience any worsening symptoms or if things get stuck.
I referred the patient to gastroenterology for a scope and evaluation of the esophagus.
I ordered another hemoglobin A1c and kidney function tests.
I also referred the patient to ophthalmology for an eye exam to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I refilled the patient's norvasc prescription and ordered a refill for metformin.
Follow-up: I will see the patient back in about a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to their treatment plan.
